senses.definition: sugar-containing molecules consisting of a nitrogenous base linked to a sugar (ribose or deoxyribose) that form the building blocks of nucleosides in DNA and RNA.
examples: Nucleosides such as adenosine are components of RNA and energy-transfer molecules.
The pharmacist explained that nucleosides are precursors to nucleotides.

senses.definition: relating to or composed of nucleosomes, the basic units of DNA packaging in chromosomes.
examples: Nucleosomal DNA is wrapped around histone proteins.
Researchers study nucleosomal patterns to understand gene regulation.

senses.definition: a unit of DNA wrapped around a core of histone proteins; the fundamental packing unit of chromatin.
examples: A nucleosome consists of about 147 base pairs of DNA wrapped around histone proteins.
Nucleosomes help compact DNA inside the cell nucleus.

senses.definition: multiple nucleosome units; the repeating structural units of chromatin.
examples: During cell division, nucleosomes must be reorganized.
The spacing of nucleosomes affects gene accessibility.

senses.definition: processes by which nucleotides or nucleosides are synthesized in living organisms.
examples: Nucleosyntheses are essential for DNA replication.
Disruptions in nucleosyntheses can lead to mutations.

senses.definition: the production of new atomic nuclei in stars or the early universe; creation of elements from protons and neutrons.
examples: Stellar nucleosynthesis creates elements like carbon and oxygen.
Primordial nucleosynthesis occurred shortly after the Big Bang.

senses.definition: relating to nucleosynthesis or the production of new atomic nuclei.
examples: Nucleosynthetic pathways determine which elements form in stars.
Researchers study nucleosynthetic yields in supernova remnants.

senses.definition: an enzyme that catalyzes the hydrolysis of nucleotides to nucleosides and phosphates.
examples: Nucleotidase activity helps recycle nucleotides in the cell.
Inhibitors of nucleotidase can affect nucleotide metabolism.

senses.definition: enzymes that break down nucleotides by removing phosphate groups.
examples: Nucleotidases regulate nucleotide pools in cells.
Altered nucleotidase activity can impact energy transfer.

senses.definition: a molecule that serves as a building block of DNA and RNA, consisting of a sugar, a phosphate group, and a nitrogenous base.
examples: DNA is made from repeating nucleotides.
Energy molecules like ATP are nucleotides.

senses.definition: the basic units of nucleic acids, consisting of a sugar, a phosphate, and a base; link together to form DNA or RNA strands.
examples: DNA strands are made of lots of nucleotides.
Mutations can change a single nucleotide.

senses.definition: the central part of a cell that contains the genetic material; also a central or most important part of something.
examples: The nucleus contains most of the cell's DNA.
The project is the nucleus of the company’s new strategy.

senses.definition: plural of nucleus; multiple central parts or centers within objects or organisms.
examples: The cells had several nucleuses observed under the microscope.
Different cores act as nucleuses for the project.

senses.definition: a species of a chemical element that has a distinct number of protons and neutrons, i.e., a particular atomic nucleus.
examples: Molybdenum-99 is a nuclide used in medical imaging.
Different nuclides of the same element have different masses.

senses.definition: different species of atomic nuclei defined by number of protons and neutrons.
examples: Various nuclides of uranium have different half-lives.
Nuclear chemistry studies nuclides and their reactions.

senses.definition: relating to nuclides or nuclei; having to do with atomic nuclei and their properties.
examples: Nuclidic stability affects isotope abundance.
Nuclidic data are used in nuclear physics.

senses.definition: a small genus of brachiopods or a taxonomic term; also a botanical Latin name; appears in some contexts as a rare or archaic term.
examples: The fossil bore a nucula shell.
Nucula is occasionally used in historical texts for certain shells.

senses.definition: a taxonomic grouping (family Nuculacea) referring to certain bivalve molluscs; in some classifications used in paleontology.
examples: Fossils from nuculacean families reveal ancient marine life.

senses.definition: a historical or rare term; could refer to a plant or biochemical compound in older literature; not common in modern standard usage.
examples: The text mentions nuculane in ancient recipes.

senses.definition: a rarely used or historical term; may appear as a proper name or obscure taxonomic reference.
examples: References to nuculania are found in old fossil records.

senses.definition: an obscure or historical term; may denote a mineral or botanical name in older texts.
examples: Nuculanium appears in limited historical literature.

senses.definition: a minor or obsolete term; may refer to small structures in plant anatomy or a small nucleus-like unit in some contexts.
examples: The nucule was described in the old botany text.

senses.definition: rare or historical term; may refer to small structures or units; not common in modern usage.
examples: The manuscript mentions nucules as tiny components.

senses.definition: relating to or resembling a nucule or nucleus; used in specialized contexts.
examples: The nuculid feature was described in the crustacean anatomy text.

senses.definition: a family name (Nuculidae) in biology, often referring to a group of small marine bivalves.
examples: Researchers studied the fossils of Nuculidae.
